review_text: Kobayashi establishes his aesthetic signature and maintains the balance of a tense and tragic narrative to interrogate the honor, bravery and hypocrisy of a feudal system, with a highly organic performance from Tatsuya Nakadai. [Full review in Spanish]
Harakiri is the symbol of resistance against authority and the inhuman tendencies of the untouchable elite against the nobility of an impoverished working class... [Full review in Spanish]
Masaki Kobayashi’s “Harakiri” illustrates the samurai code as a flawed system hiding behind a façade of honor and principle—it is undeniably the greatest anti-samurai film ever made.

The film’s condemnation of the lies systems of authority will tell to preserve their power and engrain their ideology is deeply felt and presented in a structure that leaves the viewer rife with indignation.
Both a thrilling character piece and a scathing take down of authority, Harakiri is a tour-de-force and a compelling look into the facade of institutions.
It would be wrong for me to reveal the details of the story Tsugumo tells. What I can say is that it is heartbreaking.
The 1962 single masterpiece uses ploys of the written word in picturing the past and/or far away to criticize the present.
The film has a steady, hypnotic momentum; the director, Masaki Kobayashi, wrings as much drama out of facial twitches as he does out of sword fights.
A masterful chess game, filled with many carefully constructed moves, each arranged to fit in a particular place. It's glorious to behold.
